articles.title: The AI Features of the Top Marketing Automation Platforms, Compared (2026)
articles.h1: The AI Features of the Top Marketing Automation Platforms, Compared (2026)
articles.metaDescription: Marketing automation AI compared (2026): HubSpot Breeze, Salesforce Agentforce, Marketo, ActiveCampaign and Oracle Eloqua, by how much AI acts vs. assists.
articles.ogDescription: An even-handed look at the AI in HubSpot, Salesforce, Marketo, ActiveCampaign and Oracle Eloqua — organized around how much each lets AI act versus assist.
articles.heroLede: Five major platforms now ship AI under their own brand names — but they don't mean the same thing by it. Some surface suggestions for a human to approve; others let agents send email, route journeys, and manage ad spend on their own. Here's a grounded read on each, organized around how much each one lets AI act versus assist.
articles.bodyHtml:
Every marketing automation vendor now has an "AI" page. The hard part for a buyer isn't finding AI — it's working out what the AI is actually allowed to do. There's a real difference between a feature that drafts a subject line for you to approve and an agent that continues an email thread with a prospect, reroutes a customer's journey, or adjusts ad spend in real time. Both get called "AI." Only one of them takes an irreversible action on your behalf.

So the useful way to compare these platforms isn't a feature checklist — it's a spectrum from assist (the AI proposes, a human disposes) to act (the AI executes inside the live system). Below is a fair, source-grounded summary of where each of five major platforms sits today. We're not naming an objective winner; the right answer depends on what you're trying to do and how much autonomy you're prepared to grant. At the end, we offer one lens — bounded autonomy — for deciding among any of them.

HubSpot (Breeze)

HubSpot has unified its AI under the brand Breeze, organized into three pillars: Breeze Assistant (a conversational interface that answers questions and completes tasks grounded in your CRM data and role), Breeze Agents (automated agents), and Breeze Intelligence (data enrichment and buyer-intent scoring drawn from a database of company and contact profiles to fill missing CRM fields).

The agents are where Breeze leans toward acting: the generally available lineup includes a Customer Agent that resolves inquiries and qualifies leads across channels, a Prospecting Agent that monitors accounts for buying signals and builds personalized outreach for a rep to review and send, and a Data Agent that answers questions about customers using CRM data and documents. Worth noting on maturity: several additional agents — including Company Research, Customer Health, and a Closing Agent — are listed in beta, so capability and general availability vary by feature. Full breakdown of HubSpot's Breeze AI features.

Salesforce (Agentforce Marketing / Einstein)

Salesforce sits furthest toward the act end. At Dreamforce 2025 it announced that the next generation of Marketing Cloud is now Agentforce Marketing — the Marketing Cloud line rebranded under the Agentforce brand. (If you're evaluating "Marketing Cloud," that's the current name to search for.)

The agentic features are explicit about taking action: a Two-Way Email capability lets Agentforce continue email threads or proactively start conversations, replacing one-way "Do Not Reply" sends; Journey Decisioning automatically routes each customer to their next-best journey based on real-time behavior; Adaptive Web experiences tailor web content from a live agentic conversation without manual configuration; and Paid Media Optimization monitors and manages ad performance across platforms, taking real-time actions. Underneath, Einstein persists as the foundational predictive and trust layer — a prerequisite for the agents rather than something the rebrand retired. Full breakdown of Salesforce's Agentforce and Einstein AI.

Adobe Marketo Engage

Marketo Engage is pushing hard into agentic territory, with an architecture that's notably open. The Marketo MCP Server acts as a secure hub connecting AI agents and workflows directly to Marketo data and processes, and supports connecting external AI tools via the Model Context Protocol — Adobe's documentation names clients such as Claude and OpenAI Codex, and notes any MCP-compatible client can connect. Callable Agents run inside Smart Campaign flow steps to perform tasks such as program QA, lead and data normalization, lead investigation, campaign creation, and lead import — meaning the AI can act within workflows you design.

On the assist side, an Email Designer AI Assistant suggests subject lines, writes body copy, and selects images tailored to personas or buying stages, and a product-knowledge AI Assistant answers plain-language "how do I" questions with step-by-step instructions. Marketo is also introducing a conversational interface where a chat panel becomes the primary way to browse activities and give agents commands. AI-driven Sales Qualifier and Brand Concierge features are included in the Prime and Ultimate packages. Full breakdown of Marketo Engage's agentic AI.

ActiveCampaign (Active Intelligence)

ActiveCampaign unifies its AI under Active Intelligence, spanning machine learning and generative AI. It leans toward letting AI build while keeping a human in the loop: the AI Campaign Builder creates complete campaigns from a text description, and the AI Automation Builder constructs multi-step workflows — triggers, conditions, wait steps, actions — from a stated objective.

Predictive features include Predictive Sending (timing sends to when individual contacts are likeliest to open) and Win Probability (scoring how likely a deal is to close). A March 2026 addition, AI Performance Intelligence, analyzes campaign and automation performance to flag what's over- or under-performing and the creative, timing, and audience factors behind it. Custom AI instructions let you define brand voice and strategic preferences once so they apply across recommendations and builds — and agencies can manage those across client accounts. Other features include AI-Suggested Segments, an AI Brand Kit, a Reporting Co-Pilot, and Sentiment Analysis. Full breakdown of ActiveCampaign's Active Intelligence.

Oracle Eloqua (Advanced Intelligence)

Oracle Eloqua sits closest to the assist end. Its AI is the Advanced Intelligence Cloud Service — available to all customers since June 12, 2025, but opt-in: you enable it by opening a service request with Oracle Support. The feature set is largely generative and predictive rather than autonomous: Subject Line Generation produces up to five suggestions from a prompt (replacing the discontinued Subject Line Optimization), and Content Generation creates text or variations for emails and landing pages.

On the analytics side, Fatigue Analysis estimates how email-fatigued each contact is and stores it in a system field so you can adjust frequency; Account Intelligence assigns a 0–100 Account Engagement Score with a performance dashboard; and Send Time Optimization times sends to individual engagement patterns. Development is ongoing — the Eloqua 26B release (May 2026) added a conversational experience for subject line generation, including attaching reference documents, plus admin access controls for AI features. Full breakdown of Oracle Eloqua's Advanced Intelligence.

How to actually decide

Notice that the brand names don't tell you much. "Breeze," "Agentforce," "Active Intelligence," "Advanced Intelligence" — each spans everything from a subject-line suggester to an agent that emails your prospects. So the question that separates them isn't does it have AI, it's how much is the AI allowed to act, and on what.

Our lens for that is bounded autonomy: AI should act only inside guardrails you set, and every action it takes should be visible and reversible. A clean way to pressure-test any of these platforms is the Trigger → Action → Impact frame. For each AI feature you're considering, ask:

  • Trigger — what causes it to fire, and can you see and constrain that condition?
  • Action — what exactly does it do? A suggestion you approve, or a live send / journey change / ad-spend adjustment that happens without you?
  • Impact — is the action logged, explainable, and reversible if it's wrong?

Run that against the spectrum above and the trade-off gets concrete. Eloqua's generative assists and ActiveCampaign's builders mostly produce drafts a human ships, so the blast radius of a mistake is small. Salesforce's two-way email and journey decisioning, HubSpot's outbound Prospecting Agent, and Marketo's Callable Agents can act on live contacts — higher leverage, and a stronger reason to insist on guardrails and read-only, auditable access first. Neither end is "better." The right pick is the one whose autonomy matches what you're comfortable letting run unattended — and where you can explain, bound, and reverse what it does.

Last reviewed June 2026. These platforms change their AI capabilities frequently — confirm current specifics on each vendor's own site.

Start here

Before you grant any agent autonomy, find out where you actually stand

The platform matters less than whether you can bound and reverse what its AI does. Our free Readiness Score shows you where your guardrails are strong and where AI could act unchecked — 4 minutes, no login.

Get your free Readiness Score →

  • The bounded-autonomy buyer's guide — how to grant AI just enough rope to be useful and no more.

articles.title: Sales Intelligence AI Showdown: ZoomInfo vs. Apollo vs. 6sense | Campaign Automation AI
articles.h1: ZoomInfo vs. Apollo vs. 6sense: the AI comparison.
articles.metaDescription: ZoomInfo vs Apollo vs 6sense: an even-handed look at each platform's AI approach — Copilot, AI Assistant, RevvyAI — and how to choose by what you can govern.
articles.ogTitle: Sales Intelligence AI Showdown: ZoomInfo vs. Apollo vs. 6sense
articles.ogDescription: Three data and intent players, three AI approaches. A fair, grounded comparison of ZoomInfo Copilot, Apollo's AI Assistant, and 6sense RevvyAI.
articles.heroLede: All three of these platforms now sell "AI" as a headline feature, and all three mean something a little different by it. The real buying question isn't whose AI is smartest — it's which approach fits the work you do, the data you trust it with, and the actions you're willing to let it take. Here's a grounded, vendor-by-vendor read.
articles.bodyHtml:
ZoomInfo, Apollo.io, and 6sense all live in the same neighbourhood — contact data, company data, and buyer intent — but they arrived at AI from different starting points. ZoomInfo built a deep B2B database and is layering an assistant on top of it. Apollo fused a large contact database with a sales-engagement engine and is making the whole thing answer to natural language. 6sense started from intent and predictive scoring and is putting a conversational and agent layer over that signal.

That history matters, because it shapes what each platform's AI is actually good at — and what you'll need to govern once it starts acting. Below, each vendor on its own terms, followed by the lens we'd use to decide between them.

ZoomInfo

ZoomInfo's flagship AI product is ZoomInfo Copilot, marketed as an AI sales assistant that surfaces account signals, intent data, and recommended buyers for sellers. Its Copilot Chat answers account-specific questions — pain points, prior engagements, potential contacts — and, notably, links sellers back to the source meetings, emails, and calls behind its insights, so an answer can be traced to where it came from.

On the execution side, Copilot's AI Emailer generates personalized emails at scale and lets users adjust tone and length, drawing on historical engagement and CRM data. Copilot pushes an account's key signals to sellers while they work inside Salesforce or HubSpot, and connects to Salesforce, HubSpot, Outreach, Salesloft, and Gong. It also generates participant summaries ahead of meetings, alerts sellers when an account decision-maker hasn't engaged within the first 30 days, and flags single-threaded opportunities. ZoomInfo additionally offers Copilot Workspace, an AI-powered execution layer that consolidates a seller's book of business into one place with AI agent execution for sales, account management, and customer success teams.

The shorthand: ZoomInfo leans on its data depth and a citations-back-to-source habit in Chat. For the full feature breakdown, see our ZoomInfo AI features explainer.

Apollo.io

Apollo's primary AI product is the Apollo AI Assistant, a natural-language assistant that executes go-to-market work — builds lists, drafts sequences, writes messages — directly inside the Apollo workspace rather than only suggesting actions. It operates across list building, prospect and account research, data enrichment, messaging and sequence creation, deliverability, and analytics and automation workflows. Apollo positions its AI as built on a database of over 230 million contacts plus its own sales-engagement data, which it uses to inform recommendations and personalization.

Apollo markets the Assistant within what it calls the industry's first fully agentic end-to-end GTM platform, with the assistant embedded in prospecting, sequences, and other surfaces. A distinctive piece is the AI context center: Apollo captures a customer's product, audience, and positioning once, then uses that context to shape generated lists, messages, and sequences. Apollo also ships an MCP (Model Context Protocol) integration with guided workflows, including a sequence skill, that lets external AI tools such as Claude, ChatGPT, and Perplexity act on Apollo data.

The shorthand: Apollo's pitch is breadth of in-product execution and an open hook (MCP) for outside AI tools. We unpack it further in our Apollo AI features explainer.

6sense

6sense's platform is 6sense Revenue AI, with a conversational assistant named RevvyAI that puts a natural-language interface on top of 6sense's signal and intelligence layer, so users can ask questions and get answers grounded in account data. On May 12, 2026, 6sense announced RevvyAI is in open beta for all Revenue Marketing customers and generally available for Sales Intelligence customers at no additional cost. For Sales Intelligence, RevvyAI is accessible via a Chrome Extension across LinkedIn, Sales Navigator, Salesforce, Salesloft, and Outreach (with HubSpot listed as coming soon) for account research, prospecting, and meeting prep.

6sense also brands a set of agent features under 6AI, including Email Agents that generate, send, and reply to emails grounded in real-time buyer signals, Advertising Agents, and a Sales Copilot for prospecting. Underneath sits the Signalverse, which 6sense describes as its proprietary data foundation processing roughly one trillion buyer signals per day — intent, company, and contact data — that underpins its AI applications. In Revenue Marketing, RevvyAI uses AI agents to answer questions, analyze performance, and generate insights across campaigns, keywords, and accounts via natural language.

The shorthand: 6sense leads with intent signal at scale and is wrapping it in a conversational assistant plus a growing set of agents. More in our 6sense AI features explainer.

How to actually decide

Comparing feature lists will tell you what each platform can do. It won't tell you what you can safely let it do. That's the gap our decision lens is built to close — and it applies equally to all three vendors here, with no thumb on the scale.

Each of these products now has an AI that doesn't just inform you — it acts: it writes and, in some cases, sends emails, builds and updates lists, and runs sequences. The moment software acts on your behalf, the question stops being "is it accurate?" and becomes "can it explain, bound, and reverse what it did?" That's the heart of our Trigger / Action / Impact framework and guardrail-driven automation: every action tied to the signal that triggered it, kept inside limits you set, and visible enough to undo.

So ask each vendor the same three questions:

  • Can it explain? When the AI surfaces an account or drafts a message, can you trace it back to the source signal? ZoomInfo's Copilot Chat, for instance, links answers back to source materials — a habit worth asking every vendor to match.
  • Can it bound? Where do you set the limits on what it sends, enriches, or changes without a human in the loop? An agent that emails or runs sequences on your behalf needs explicit guardrails, not just an on/off switch.
  • Can it reverse? After it acts, where's the log, and how do you undo a single action? Read-only and auditable access first; autonomy earned step by step.

None of these platforms is the universal "winner" — the honest answer is best-for-which-buyer. If your edge is data depth and you want answers you can trace, ZoomInfo's approach fits. If you want one workspace where the AI does the GTM work and an open hook for outside tools, Apollo's breadth fits. If intent signal is your starting point and you want a conversational layer plus agents over it, 6sense fits. The deciding factor is which one lets you keep the explain-bound-reverse controls you need.

Last reviewed June 2026. These platforms change their AI capabilities frequently — confirm current specifics on each vendor's own site.

Start here

Before you pick a sales-intelligence AI, check what you can govern.

The free Readiness Score checks whether your data, processes, and guardrails can support an AI that acts — across any of these platforms — or where you should start read-only first. Four minutes, no login.

Get your free Readiness Score →

  • The bounded-autonomy buyer's guide — how to let AI act without losing control of your account.
  • How to evaluate an AI marketing automation platform — nine criteria that cut through the demo.

articles.title: Which CRM Has the Best AI? Salesforce vs. HubSpot in 2026
articles.h1: Which CRM Has the Best AI? Salesforce vs. HubSpot in 2026
articles.metaDescription: Salesforce vs HubSpot AI in 2026: a neutral, even-handed look at Agentforce 360 and Breeze to help you decide which CRM AI best fits your team and goals.
articles.ogTitle: Salesforce vs HubSpot AI: Agentforce vs Breeze in 2026
articles.ogDescription: A grounded, vendor-neutral comparison of Agentforce 360 and Breeze, plus a bounded-autonomy lens for deciding which CRM AI is best for your team.
articles.heroLede: Agentforce 360 and Breeze are both mature, brand-consolidated AI platforms in 2026. The honest answer to "which is best" depends entirely on who's buying — here's how to figure out which one fits you.
articles.bodyHtml:
"Which CRM has the best AI?" is the wrong question to ask in 2026. Both Salesforce and HubSpot now ship active, brand-consolidated AI platforms — Agentforce on one side, Breeze on the other — and both can build autonomous agents, draft content, enrich records, and act inside your CRM. There is no single objective winner. There is only a better fit for your team, your data, and your appetite for letting software act on your behalf.

So the useful question becomes: best for which buyer? A revenue team standing on a large, governed enterprise data estate weighs different things than a marketing team that wants powerful AI without a heavy build. Below is a fair, grounded look at each vendor's approach, followed by the lens we use to help buyers decide.

Salesforce (Agentforce 360 / Einstein)

Salesforce's AI is now unified under Agentforce, announced as the integrated "Agentforce 360" platform on October 13, 2025 and positioned as a system to connect humans and AI agents in one place. The older "Einstein" name lives on mainly as the Einstein Trust Layer — a governance layer with dynamic grounding, data masking, zero data retention agreements with model providers, and toxicity scoring of prompts and responses.

Agentforce agents are driven by the Atlas Reasoning Engine, which Salesforce describes as how an agent understands a request, plans, decides, and acts. Notably, Atlas can be powered by third-party models, including Anthropic's Claude hosted on Amazon Bedrock. Builders work in a low-code Agent Builder using existing Salesforce tools — Flows, Prompts, Apex, and MuleSoft APIs — with "Hybrid Reasoning" and Agent Script for combining deterministic workflows with LLM reasoning and defining guardrails. Salesforce also ships pre-built agents such as a Service Agent and a Sales Development Representative (SDR) agent, plus embedded agents across Sales, Service, Marketing, and Commerce clouds. Data 360 supplies the context, including access to unstructured content like PDFs and diagrams, and Tableau Semantics mapping data to business language.

The picture this paints is a platform aimed at organizations that already live in the Salesforce ecosystem and want to compose agents over a large, governed data estate. For the full breakdown, see our Salesforce AI features explainer.

HubSpot (Breeze)

HubSpot's AI is unified under Breeze, which spans its Smart CRM and is organized into four pieces: Breeze Agents (specialized AI agents), Breeze Assistant (an in-app companion with CRM access for content, campaigns, and analysis), Breeze Studio (the control center for managing and customizing agents), and a Breeze Marketplace for discovering and installing agents.

The agent lineup includes a Customer Agent (always-on support across channels with human handoff), a Prospecting Agent (identifies in-market accounts from buying signals, builds contact lists, and prepares personalized outreach), and a Data Agent that answers questions by analyzing CRM data, conversations, documents, and web sources — alongside beta agents such as Customer Health, Company Research, and Closing agents. A note on status: HubSpot's own product page lists six Breeze Agents with several in beta, while some third-party 2026 guides describe three or four as generally available, so treat per-agent GA status as fluid. Separately, Breeze Intelligence handles data enrichment and buyer intent — one-click enrichment using a published set of firmographic, demographic, and technographic attributes drawn from a continually refreshed dataset of company and buyer profiles, plus Buyer Intent and dynamic Form Shortening. Breeze Intelligence traces back to HubSpot's 2023 acquisition of Clearbit, introduced under the Breeze brand in 2024.

This leans toward teams that want capable, packaged AI with enrichment built in and less assembly required. For more, read our HubSpot AI features explainer.

How to actually decide

Feature checklists won't settle this, because both platforms are moving fast and both can plausibly do what you need. The deciding question isn't "which has more agents" — it's "which one will act inside the bounds I set, and let me see and undo what it did." That's the lens we apply: bounded autonomy, where AI acts only inside guardrails the buyer defines, and every action is visible and reversible.

We frame each action as a Trigger, Action, Impact chain — what set the agent off, what it did, and what changed as a result. Ask any vendor, Salesforce or HubSpot, the same three questions:

  • Can it explain? When an agent reaches a decision, can you trace the trigger and the reasoning, not just the output?
  • Can you bound it? Can you constrain what it's allowed to touch — ideally starting with read-only, auditable access before you grant it the ability to write or send?
  • Can you reverse it? If an agent emails the wrong segment or updates the wrong records, how fast and how cleanly can you roll it back?

Both Salesforce and HubSpot ship governance machinery — the Einstein Trust Layer, Breeze Studio's controls — and both deserve a fair hearing on these questions. The point isn't that either is unsafe; it's that the answers, applied to your workflows, tell you more than any feature comparison. Our guardrail-driven automation approach starts there: prove the guardrails before you grant autonomy.

Last reviewed June 2026. These platforms change their AI capabilities frequently — confirm current specifics on each vendor's own site.

Start here

Before you pick Agentforce or Breeze, find out what your team is actually ready to automate

The platform matters less than how much autonomy you can safely grant on day one. Our free Readiness Score shows you where you stand — 4 minutes, no login.

Get your free Readiness Score →

Keep reading

  • AI agents in your CRM — what changes when agents start acting inside your system of record
  • The bounded-autonomy buyer's guide — how to grant AI autonomy you can see, bound, and reverse
  • Predictive vs. agentic AI in marketing — telling scoring apart from software that takes action

articles.title: Eloqua vs. Marketo vs. HubSpot: Whose AI Fits Enterprise B2B?
articles.h1: Eloqua vs. Marketo vs. HubSpot: Whose AI Fits Enterprise B2B?
articles.metaDescription: Eloqua vs marketo vs hubspot for enterprise B2B: an even-handed look at each platform's AI, weighing governance, autonomy, and time-to-value before you buy.
articles.ogTitle: Eloqua vs. Marketo vs. HubSpot: Whose AI Fits Enterprise B2B?
articles.ogDescription: A neutral three-way comparison of Oracle Eloqua, Adobe Marketo Engage, and HubSpot Breeze AI — and how to decide using a bounded-autonomy lens.
articles.heroLede: Three mature platforms, three very different bets on what AI should do inside your marketing engine. Here's a grounded look at each — and the questions that actually separate them for an enterprise B2B buyer.
articles.bodyHtml:
If you're evaluating Oracle Eloqua, Adobe Marketo Engage, and HubSpot for an enterprise B2B program, the AI feature lists will blur together fast. Every vendor promises generative content, predictive scoring, and "agents." The real buying question isn't who has AI — all three do — it's what kind of AI fits the way your team works, your governance requirements, and how quickly you need to see value.

Those three pressures pull in different directions. Predictive AI that scores and ranks is easy to govern but waits for a human to act. Generative AI speeds up content but needs review. Agentic AI that takes actions on its own can compress time-to-value — and raises the most governance questions. Below is an even-handed read of where each platform sits, based on each vendor's own documentation, followed by a decision lens you can apply to all three.

Oracle Eloqua

Eloqua's AI splits cleanly into two halves. Its predictive capabilities are bundled as the Advanced Intelligence Cloud Service, which Oracle's documentation says became available to all customers effective June 12, 2025 (enabled via service request). That suite includes Fatigue Analysis, which generates a per-contact Fatigue Level stored in a system field you can use across segmentation, orchestration, personalization, lead scoring, and analytics to tune message frequency; Account Intelligence, which assigns a 0–100 Account Engagement Score and surfaces activity on an Account Performance dashboard; and Send Time Optimization, which delivers each contact's email when that contact has historically been most attentive.

On the generative side, Eloqua now has built-in Subject Line Generation (up to five suggestions from a text prompt, replacing the end-of-life Subject Line Optimization feature) and Content Generation for text and variations across both emails and landing pages. The pattern here is largely predict-and-suggest: the AI scores, flags, and drafts, while the marketer stays in the loop on the action. For account-centric enterprise teams already in the Oracle stack, that's a familiar, governable shape. See our full Oracle Eloqua AI features breakdown for detail.

Adobe Marketo Engage

Marketo is now Adobe Marketo Engage — Adobe-branded and woven into Adobe's wider AI tooling. Its Email Designer AI Assistant uses Adobe's generative AI, including Adobe Firefly for image generation plus a prompt library, to produce subject lines, preheaders, section content, and entire emails, with optional alternate image models. Predictive Audiences adds AI/ML Smart List filters such as "Likelihood to Unsubscribe," "Lookalike of Program Members," and "Lookalike of Smart List Members" (the lookalike filters weigh 50+ factors, included with Prime or Ultimate bundles). Predictive Content — formerly ContentAI, and an add-on — recommends the most relevant content to web visitors and email recipients.

Marketo also pushes generative AI into engagement surfaces: Dynamic Chat generates real-time conversation summaries and a pre-approved Response Library, and Interactive Webinars auto-generate content summaries, an FAQ, and video chapters from transcripts. Notably, Adobe is rolling out agentic AI for Marketo Engage — a conversational interface with embedded agents for tasks like importing lead lists while flagging duplicates, standardizing values, and cleaning records. That moves Marketo further toward AI that acts, not just suggests. Read more in our Marketo AI features overview.

HubSpot

HubSpot frames its AI as Breeze, which it describes as the AI agents and features that power the platform, organized into three layers. Breeze Assistant is a conversational interface that answers questions and completes tasks using CRM data, learned content sources, and role-aware context across desktop and mobile. Breeze Agents are automated, customizable workflows installed from the HubSpot Marketplace or built in Breeze Studio — the catalog includes a Customer Agent, Prospecting Agent, Data Agent, Company Research Agent, and Customer Health Agent. Breeze Intelligence handles data enrichment, auto-populating 40+ record details (company size, industry, revenue, and more) by drawing on a database of 200M+ company and contact profiles.

Breeze Intelligence also provides buyer intent by matching website-visitor IP addresses to companies and flagging high-intent pages (available on Starter, Professional, and Enterprise subscriptions, excluding Revenue Hub), and form shortening that drops fields when the data already exists. Of the three, HubSpot leans hardest into agentic, task-completing AI and CRM-grounded enrichment — which can shorten time-to-value, and is also where governance questions concentrate. Our HubSpot AI features page goes deeper.

How to actually decide

Feature parity is a trap. All three can draft an email and score a lead; the meaningful differences are in autonomy and control. The lens we apply at campaignautomation.ai is bounded autonomy: AI should act only inside guardrails you set, and every action it takes should be visible and reversible. That's not a knock on any vendor — it's a way to compare them on the dimension that actually bites in production.

For each AI capability you're considering — Eloqua's Fatigue Level writes, Marketo's record-cleaning agents, HubSpot's Breeze Agents — run it through Trigger, Action, Impact: What triggers it? What action does it take, and is that action a suggestion a human approves or an autonomous change to your data and sends? What's the impact if it's wrong — and can you see it and roll it back? Then ask three questions of every platform on your shortlist:

  • Can it explain itself? When the AI scores an account 0–100 or recommends content, can you trace why?
  • Can you bound it? Can you set guardrails on what it's allowed to touch, and start with read-only or suggestion-only access before granting it the keys?
  • Can you reverse it? If an agent cleans records or an optimizer reshapes a send, is every action logged and undoable?

There's no universal winner here — there's a best fit for your risk tolerance and stack. Account-centric Oracle shops, Adobe-ecosystem teams, and CRM-first orgs will each weigh these answers differently. The point is to decide on autonomy and governance deliberately, not by feature checkbox.

Last reviewed June 2026. These platforms change their AI capabilities frequently — confirm current specifics on each vendor's own site.
